channel.title: Ali Safari Blog
channel.description: Thoughts on IS theory, AI, technology, and comps studying.

item.title: 5G Is Not About Faster Phones. Here Is What Enterprises Actually Care About.
item.description: Private 5G networks let enterprises build their own cellular infrastructure inside a factory or warehouse. Most organizations are still figuring out what to do with that.

item.title: Bought a $2M Platform, Nobody Learned a Thing
item.description: Organizations buy expensive analytics platforms and learn nothing. The theory of absorptive capacity explains why prior knowledge, not capital, determines whether new technology becomes intelligence or noise.

item.title: Action Research in IS: When the Researcher Is Also the Intervener
item.description: Action research asks you to help solve a real problem while studying it. The dual role is genuinely difficult, and the rigor vs. relevance tension never fully resolves.

item.title: People Use Tools Inside Activity Systems, Not in a Vacuum
item.description: Activity theory says you cannot understand tool use without the full activity system around it. The same tool in two systems means two completely different things.

item.title: Actor-Network Theory: Why Your ERP System Is an Actor Too
item.description: ANT treats software, databases, and infrastructure as actants with the same analytical standing as humans. In IS research, that changes everything about how we study implementation.

item.title: The Gap Between What a System Can Do and What People Do With It
item.description: Affordance actualization explains why two organizations using the same system get completely different results. The technology affords possibilities. People and context determine what happens.

item.title: The Door Handle Is Not for Everyone
item.description: An affordance is not a feature. It is an action possibility for a specific actor with a specific goal in a specific context. Most IS papers get this wrong.

item.title: When the Algorithm Fails, Nobody Takes the Call
item.description: Agency theory explains IT outsourcing and governance through principals, agents, and monitoring. AI rewrites the problem in a way the original framework did not anticipate.

item.title: The Internet of Agents Will Be Built by Platforms That Own the Protocol
item.description: The platform that controls the agent communication protocol will control the Internet of Agents, just as Google owned search and Apple owned mobile.

item.title: Agile at Enterprise Scale: Why It Is Harder Than It Looks
item.description: Scaling agile to 500 people across three time zones is a different problem entirely. The frameworks meant to help often just create new bureaucracy.

item.title: AI Adoption Follows the S-Curve. Most Organizations Are Still in the Chasm.
item.description: Rogers showed that innovation adoption follows an S-curve. Enterprise AI is not stalling. It is exactly where diffusion theory predicts.

item.title: AI Adoption Spreads Through Social Networks, Not Tech Budgets
item.description: Rogers, Granovetter, and Burt explain why the department with the AI champion adopts faster while the department across the building with the same budget does not.

item.title: Why AI Adoption Produces ROI for Some and a Headache for Others
item.description: TOE from Tornatzky and Fleischer explains why most AI investments fail while a few transform organizations: the three contexts must align.

item.title: AI Agents Create a Principal-Agent Problem at Machine Speed
item.description: The AI alignment problem is principal-agent theory with a new degree of freedom: the agent can act faster than human oversight can keep up.

item.title: AI Creates an Ambidexterity Challenge Every Organization Is Failing
item.description: Organizations use AI to optimize existing processes while calling it transformation. March (1991) would call this pure exploitation, and the exploration gap is getting dangerous.

item.title: AI Augmentation vs. AI Automation: Getting the Frame Right
item.description: There are two fundamentally different visions of AI in the workplace, and the IS research frame you choose changes everything about what you study and what you find.

item.title: AI Chatbots Are the Richest Medium That Has Never Existed
item.description: AI chatbots feel like a face to face conversation but function like a search engine with a confidence problem. Media richness theory explains why that mismatch is dangerous.

item.title: Copilot Generated More Code and More Bugs
item.description: Thirty percent acceptance is adoption, not effective use. Burton-Jones and Grange defined effective use as faithful domain representation, not output.

item.title: When AI Is a Colleague, Not a Tool: Structuration in the Agent Era
item.description: Multiagent AI systems are not just tools being used. They act, and their outputs become part of the organizational structure that shapes the next cycle.

item.title: AI's Energy Problem Is a Sociotechnical Systems Problem
item.description: Training AI models consumes staggering amounts of energy. Trist and Bamforth showed in 1951 why optimizing only the technical subsystem creates a collapse.

item.title: AI Ethics Boards That Never Blocked a Project
item.description: Meyer and Rowan predicted that organizations adopt structures for legitimacy, not performance. AI ethics boards are the most direct example I have seen.

item.title: AI Adopted the Way the Garbage Can Model Predicts
item.description: Cohen, March and Olsen showed that solutions search for problems, not the other way around. Most organizations adopted AI exactly that way between 2023 and 2025.

item.title: AI Hallucination Is a Trust Problem, Not a Technical One
item.description: Hallucination is framed as a retrieval bug to be engineered away. Lee and See would call it a trust calibration failure, and that framing changes the solution entirely.

item.title: Why Doctors Still Resist Better AI: Identity, Not Accuracy
item.description: AI outperforms radiologists at detection but hospitals do not adopt it. Identity theory explains why. The tools that succeed will verify professional identity, not threaten it.

item.title: The Gap Between Fair Metrics and Fair Experience (Amazon's AI Hiring Edition)
item.description: Kattnig et al. (2024) showed a model can satisfy every fairness metric and still feel deeply unfair. Amazon's AI hiring tool proved it with real damage.

item.title: AI Implementation Failures Are Sociotechnical, Not Technical
item.description: Bostrom and Heinen said in 1977 that MIS failures are sociotechnical. Half a century later, AI implementation fails the same way.

item.title: AI and the Labor Market: What IS Research Can Contribute
item.description: Economists study macro displacement. IS researchers can study what actually happens inside organizations when AI changes specific jobs, roles, and power structures.

item.title: Critical Realism Explains Why AI Models Are Not the Mechanisms They Simulate
item.description: AI models predict patterns in observed data but never access the generative mechanisms that produce them. Critical realism tells us why that ceiling is not a technical problem.

item.title: Tiny Teams, Big Questions: What Happens When AI Writes the Code
item.description: Gartner says two-person AI-augmented teams will deliver what twenty-person teams do today. Baird and Maruping predicted this years ago. The real question is not team size but delegation governance.

item.title: AI Has The Strongest Network Effects Ever, And That Is a Problem
item.description: Network effects theory explains why AI markets concentrate around a few providers and why the data flywheel makes it nearly impossible for new entrants to catch up.

item.title: We Learn to Use AI by Watching Others, and That Is Why Adoption Is Uneven
item.description: Social cognitive theory predicts that AI adoption clusters not by access but by observation: people adopt when they see peers succeed with AI, and the gap between teams with visible champions and those without will persist.

item.title: AI Output Is a Boundary Object. That Is Why It Is So Hard to Govern.
item.description: Star and Griesemer defined boundary objects in 1989. AI output fits the definition perfectly. And that is why every governance policy that treats it as a single type of thing fails.

item.title: AI Is Not a Tool. It Is a Participant in the Work System.
item.description: Alter's work system framework already has a slot for AI. It is not the technology element. It is the participant element.

item.title: Why AI Pilots Don't Become Products
item.description: Almost every large organization has run an AI pilot. Very few of those pilots make it to production. That gap is not a data science problem.

item.title: Your AI Policy Is Just Your Old IT Policy With AI Pasted In
item.description: Almost every AI policy is a find-and-replaced IT acceptable-use policy. DiMaggio and Powell explain why that pattern is not lazy. It is institutional isomorphism.

item.title: AI Regulation: Why Every Country Is Writing Different Rules
item.description: The EU has comprehensive legislation. The US has executive orders. China has sector-specific rules. The UK has no AI-specific law. Organizations operating globally must navigate all four simultaneously.

item.title: AI Is Forcing IS Research Methods to Evolve. Here Is What Should Change.
item.description: IS research methods assume technology is stable and humans adapt. AI inverts that assumption, and our methods have not caught up.

item.title: AI Safety Frameworks Are PMT Without Using the Name
item.description: AI safety warnings raise threat appraisal sky high. Without coping appraisal, they produce fear control, not safety.

item.title: AI Security Platforms Are Governance Boundary Resources
item.description: When 80% of unauthorized AI use is internal policy violations, AI security platforms are governance products, not security products.

item.title: AI Self-Efficacy Is the New Digital Divide
item.description: Access to AI tools is not the barrier. The belief that you can use them effectively is what separates who gains and who falls behind.

item.title: AI Supercomputing Is Not a Moat
item.description: Every major cloud vendor now sells GPU clusters. The hardware is commodity. The capability to use it is not. Here is what Carr and Barney teach us about AI moats.

item.title: When AI Gets It Wrong, Who Is Responsible?
item.description: The accountability gap in algorithmic decision-making is an IS governance problem, not just a legal one. And the EU AI Act is forcing organizations to confront it.

item.title: AI Is Not a Technology. It Is a Task-Technology Fit Problem.
item.description: Goodhue and Thompson said performance follows fit, not features. AI is a family of tools, and the wrong one for the task will fail the same way every time.

item.title: AI Training Data Made the Knowledge-Based View Visible
item.description: The New York Times lawsuit against OpenAI is the Knowledge-Based View of the firm becoming visible in court and in every training data licensing negotiation.

item.title: AI Lowers the Cost of Coordination, So Why Are Organizations Still Siloed?
item.description: Transaction cost economics predicts that when AI cuts coordination costs, organizations should restructure. But they are not. What gives?

item.title: After AI Hallucinates, How Does It Earn Trust Back?
item.description: AI trust repair is not about rebuilding a relationship. It is about recalibration. The IS trust literature has been explaining this for two decades.

item.title: AI Vendor Concentration Creates a New Resource Dependency
item.description: Pfeffer and Salancik said dependence rises when resources are critical and concentrated. AI model providers are the most concentrated critical resource in IS history.

item.title: Algorithmic Bias: When the Model Is the Policy
item.description: When an algorithm screens job applicants or recommends bail conditions, it is not neutral infrastructure. It is policy, running at scale, with no judgment calls.

item.title: The API Economy and Why Your Software Is Now a Platform
item.description: APIs let companies monetize capabilities rather than products. The strategic logic is platform theory, and the dependency chain it creates is intentional.

item.title: Strategy Is What Your Organization Actually Pays Attention To
item.description: Ocasio's attention-based view says firm behavior follows what executives notice, not what they intend. Attention is the scarcest organizational resource.
